post.title: MCP Video Gen: I Tried to Build a Video Generation Server. It Was Harder Than I Thought.
post.content:
When I started building MCP Video Gen, I thought: how hard can it be? Wrap a video generation API, add some MCP protocol, done. Three weeks later, I had rewritten the architecture four times.

The Problem I Was Trying to Solve

I wanted AI agents to create videos. Not just describe them, but actually generate them. You know those moments when an AI says imagine a sunset over mountains? I wanted it to actually make that sunset.

MCP Video Gen was my attempt to bridge that gap using the Model Context Protocol.

Architecture Iteration 1: The Naive Approach

My first design was straightforward:
- Receive text prompt from MCP client
- Call video generation API
- Return video URL

Simple, right? Wrong.

The first problem: video generation takes time. We are talking 30 seconds to 5 minutes depending on complexity. MCP protocol expects responses within reasonable timeframes. My initial synchronous design would timeout constantly.

Architecture Iteration 2: Async with Polling

I added a job queue:
- Submit prompt, get job ID
- Client polls for completion
- Return video when ready

This worked better, but introduced a new problem: state management. Where do I store job status? How do I handle crashed jobs? What happens when the API rate limits me mid-generation?

The State Problem

MCP servers are supposed to be stateless-ish. But video generation requires tracking:
- Job status (pending, processing, complete, failed)
- Generation progress (some APIs provide this)
- Generated video storage
- Cleanup of old videos

I ended up using a simple SQLite database for job tracking and a file-based storage for generated videos. Not elegant, but reliable.

Architecture Iteration 3: Event-Driven Design

The third iteration introduced webhooks:
- Submit prompt
- API calls webhook when complete
- Store result, notify client

This was cleaner, but required a publicly accessible endpoint. For local development, I needed ngrok or similar tunneling. Added complexity.

The Prompt Engineering Challenge

Here is something I did not expect: prompt quality varies dramatically between video APIs.

Some APIs want detailed descriptions. Others prefer simple subjects. Some understand cinematic while others ignore style keywords entirely.

In MCP Video Gen, I added a prompt transformation layer that adapts the user input to each API language. This involved:

1. Analyzing each API successful prompts
2. Building transformation rules
3. Testing and refining

A prompt like a dog running in the park might become:
- API A: A playful golden retriever running through a sunlit park, slow motion, cinematic, 4K
- API B: Dog running park
- API C: Playful dog, outdoor setting, dynamic movement

The Cost Reality Check

Video generation is expensive. I learned this the hard way.

A single 5-second video can cost anywhere from 0.05 to 2.00 dollars depending on quality and API. During development, I accidentally burned through 50 dollars in test generations.

I added cost estimation and budget controls:
- Estimate cost before generation
- Daily and monthly budget limits
- Alert when approaching limits

Error Handling: The Unsexy Reality

Video APIs fail. A lot.

- Rate limits (you hit these fast)
- Content policy violations (surprising what gets flagged)
- API outages
- Generation failures (model hallucinates, produces broken video)

In MCP Video Gen, I implemented exponential backoff for retries, detailed error messages for users, and fallback to alternative APIs when available.

The MCP Protocol Learning Curve

Understanding MCP itself was a journey. It is not just JSON-RPC; there is a specific way to:
- Register tools and their schemas
- Handle resource URIs
- Manage client-server communication
- Deal with capability negotiation

The protocol is well-designed, but the documentation is still evolving. I spent hours reading the spec and experimenting.

What I Would Do Differently

If I were starting MCP Video Gen today:

1. Start with async design - Do not even try synchronous video generation
2. Budget for API costs early - Set up spending alerts immediately
3. Build prompt translation from day one - Each API speaks its own language
4. Implement proper cleanup - Old videos pile up fast
5. Add generation preview - Let users see what they are getting into

The Current State

MCP Video Gen now handles:
- Multiple video generation APIs (with fallback)
- Async job processing with status updates
- Prompt transformation per API
- Cost tracking and budgets
- Comprehensive error handling

It is not perfect, but it works.

What I Am Still Figuring Out

- How to provide progress updates in real-time
- Whether to add video editing capabilities
- How to handle very long videos (longer than 1 minute)
- Better prompt optimization using AI

Lessons for Fellow Builders

If you are building something similar:

1. Video generation is slower than you think - design for patience
2. API costs add up fast - monitor from day one
3. Prompt engineering is API-specific - build translation layers
4. State management matters - even in stateless systems
5. Error messages are UX - make them helpful

Building MCP Video Gen taught me that wrapping an API is never just wrapping an API. Every integration has depth, complexity, and surprises.

For those curious: search MCP Video Gen or check kevinten-ai on GitHub.

What is your experience with video generation APIs? Which ones have you tried, and what surprised you?
